title: Dormant volcanoes may not be so dormant
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Vaginal microbes make brain-boosting lipid, South America’s settlement was surprisingly complex, and more
Brought to you by Science Custom Publishing
View this email in your browser
23 April 2026
Today’s Visualized examines a method for growing initially flat sheets of cells that fold into specific shapes. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including not-so-sleepy volcanoes and waves of human migration in South America.
Microbiology  |  News from Science
Vaginal microbes make a brain-boosting lipid on babies’ skin
Infants delivered vaginally have different microbiota from those delivered by C-section, but C-section babies can get a dose of their mothers’ flora applied to their mouths, skin and noses, mimicking a natural process that is thought to confer health benefits. While it’s been long assumed that the gut is where these microbes do most of their work, new research points to the skin as an important actor.

In human and mouse studies, two species of bacteria common in the vagina were seen acting in concert to synthesize a lipid on the skin of newborns. That lipid, the researchers showed in mice pups, gets absorbed through the soft skin; its makes its way to the brain, and acts on a signaling pathway involved in early motor development.

Importantly, the lipid is produced on the skins of C-section babies treated with vaginal microbial transfer, also called vaginal seeding. The study is the first to propose a mechanism for why treated infants see slightly better neurodevelopmental outcomes than untreated ones.

Immunologist Smita Gopinath, who was not involved in the work, called the study “very, very cool.” She noted that the findings help explain why certain vaginal bacteria become more abundant during pregnancy, though they don’t reside long in infants’ guts. “Skin just makes sense as a way for them to mediate [an effect] on the baby,” she said.
Read the full story
Volanology  |  Science Advamces
Awake or sleeping?
Methana’s central volcano may not be as restful as researchers thought.  kempanik via Wikimedia Commons | CC0
Unreliable field weather forecasts and faulty equipment aren’t the only things giving scientists trust issues. New research shows that dormant volcanoes may not be so sleepy after all.

Scientists have long presumed Greece’s Methana volcano to be extinct, since records show no ash clouds or signs of volcanic activity over thousands of years. To look deeper into the past, researchers assessed 700,000 years of the volcano’s history using tiny zircon crystals, which cool alongside magma and trap information about a volcano’s evolution. To their surprise, the crystals showed that Methana has been producing magma nearly continuously—including during a 100,000-year-long stretch without eruptions.

The researchers think this hidden activity is due to how plate tectonics carries the base of the ocean floor down into the mantle beneath Methana and makes the magma extra water-rich. The excess water triggers crystals to form in the magma, which makes the magma sluggish and less likely to erupt. The team thinks a similarly deceptive process could be occurring at volcanoes above other subduction zones around the world.

“For volcano hazard authorities … this [result] means re-evaluating the threat level of volcanoes that have been quiet for tens of thousands of years but show periodic signs of magmatic unrest,” said author Olivier Bachmann in a statement.
Read the paper
Human GEnetics  |  News from Science
South America’s surprisingly complex settlement
In the long history of global human settlement, South America was the end of the line. Humans are thought to have expanded into the continent around 15,000 years ago. Many scientists once assumed this settlement was straightforward, with a mostly homogenous population sweeping south from North America and learning to live amongst South America’s lush jungles and windswept altiplano.

But a study now suggests human migration onto the continent was far more complex. Previous work had established that there were at least two pulses of people into South America: The initial dispersal, and then a second one about 9000 years ago. The new study, based on an analysis of modern and ancient genomes from living people from north Mexico to southern Argentina, identifies a third pulse about 1300 years ago that spread widely across the continent and even into the Caribbean—though researchers don’t know exactly where they came from or who were their closest relatives.

The work also shines light on another genetic mystery: Why do scientists detect traces of Australasian ancestry in some ancient and modern South American genomes? The study’s lead author, geneticist Tábita Hünemeier, suspects people carrying this ancestry were among several populations that lived for thousands of years in Beringia, the now-drowned landmass that connected eastern Siberia to Alaska. As they made their way southward, they carried that ancestry with them.

The fact that these genes remained relatively rare but stable over a 10,000-year time span indicates they may have offered some evolutionary advantages in certain circumstances—but it’s so far unclear what those might have been.
Read the full story
SPONSORED
Designing targeted therapies for cancer's next move
Targeted therapies are reshaping cancer care, but tumors evolve to resist them. In this Q&A, Jeff Settleman of Pfizer Oncology discusses strategies to anticipate resistance, target multiple pathways, and design more precise treatments.
Read more
Visualized
A planar sheet of cells grown on a protein matrix patterned with defects folds into a 3D shape upon release.  GUILLAMAT ET AL./Science (2026)
 
When cells grow together, they often align with their neighbors, forming liquid crystal-like patterns known as “nematic order.” But that order isn’t perfect, and at certain points, the alignment can break down, creating “topological defects”: small regions where cells swirl or fan out instead of lining up cleanly. And according to new research published in Science, what looks like a defect can be used strategically as built-in instructions for shaping larger structures . Instead of growing upward, cells can store and release stress in certain places along a previously flat sheetlike structure, forming a bowl, a ridge, or a fold.

In experiments, researchers grew fibroblast cells on soft surfaces patterned with specific defect arrangements. The cells followed those cues, organizing themselves into ordered, but slightly strained, configurations. Those defects then generated localized stress fields across the sheet, shaped by the position and orientation of each defect. When the researchers released the cells from the surface, the stored stresses relaxed and the flat sheet buckled into 3D shapes.
Proteins are patterned on a soft surface to position topological defects (1), cells then align to the pattern, organizing around those defects (2). The arrangement generates internal stress across the sheet (3), which when released, drives the sheet to buckle into a 3D shape (4).  N. BURGESS/SCIENCe
 
Two defects produced a bowl, four created a threefold symmetric fold, and six led to more complex geometries, including saddlelike structures. “This demonstrates that living cells and tissues can be programmed to form a desired structure by imposing geometric features,” explain John Dunlop and Łucja Kowalewska in a related Perspective—“an approach that could be useful for regenerative medicine and designing materials for robotics that can change shape and properties in response to external stimuli.”

The idea may also extend beyond lab-grown tissues, as topological defects are unavoidable on curved surfaces—a constraint captured by the so-called “hairy ball” theorem—and similar patterns have been observed in systems ranging from bacterial swarms to biological membranes.
Read the Science Paper
Read the perspective
Et Cetera
More than just support
A whole brain 3D map of astrocytes—support cells in the brain—reveals unexpected long-distance connections. “It’s a secret subway system we didn’t know was there,” said one neuroscientist. “This opens up a whole new avenue of investigation.”
Nature Paper  |  Read more at Nature
The sound of rain
The sound of raindrops falling induced rice seeds to sprout 30% to 40% faster than those kept in quiet conditions. “What this study is saying is that the seeds can sense sound in ways that can help them survive,” said one of the researchers.
Scientific Reports Paper  |  Read more at Scientific American
Muddy diet
Macaques in Gibraltar are routinely fed—and steal—snacks from tourists. Researchers think they also eat lots of dirt to offset the junk food. “We think that eating this junk food disrupts the composition of the microbiome and we know that bacteria and minerals in soil can help recompose the microbiome and alleviate the negative effects,” one expert said. “We think there’s a protective effect of the soil.”
Scientific Reports Paper  |  Read more at The Guardian
70%
The percentage of more than 16,000 people who believe at least one of six unproven health claims
In a global survey of 16 countries, the vast majority of people said that some or many claims—such as that raw milk is healthier than pasteurized or that vaccines are used for population control—were true. The results challenge the popular notion that extreme health views are held by a select few because of ideology or politics. “This is not like a small problematic group,” noted the head of research at the Edelman Trust Institute, which conducted the survey.
Read more at Nature
Last but not least
I’m bad enough at ping-pong. I don’t need to know I could be easily beaten by a robot.

And for dedicated daily readers, the 18 LLM-associated words in yesterday’s The Life Academic are the 12 the Scientometrics paper tracked (unveil, underscore, pivotal, delve, meticulous, interplay, foster, showcase, bolster, intricate, heighten, and nuance) as well as six others mentioned (innovative, explore, commendable, enhance, realm, and crucial).
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Jennie Smith, Hannah Richter, Michael Price, and Ana Georgescu

items.title: points to the skin as an important actor
Methana has been producing magma nearly continuously
human migration onto the continent was far more complex
what looks like a defect can be used strategically as built-in instructions for shaping larger structures
This demonstrates that living cells and tissues can be programmed to form a desired structure by imposing geometric features
I could be easily beaten by a robot

raw_metadata.subject: Dormant volcanoes may not be so dormant
